title: Python Variables — Quick Reference Card
printNote: This cheat sheet covers the complete syntax for Python variables. Print it and keep it on your desk during the first two weeks of learning Python.

entries.description: Create a variable and assign a value
entries.commonMistake: Do not use 'int name = 95' — Python needs no type keyword

entries.description: Assign the same value to multiple names at once
entries.commonMistake: All three names point to the SAME object (fine for immutables, be careful with mutables)

entries.description: Assign multiple values in one line (tuple unpacking)
entries.commonMistake: Number of names must match number of values, else ValueError

entries.description: Swap two variables without a temporary variable
entries.commonMistake: In other languages this needs: temp = a; a = b; b = temp

entries.description: Star unpacking — captures remaining items into a list
entries.commonMistake: Only one starred name allowed per unpacking expression

entries.description: Assign and return in one expression (Python 3.8+)
entries.commonMistake: Cannot use := as a standalone statement — must be inside an expression

entries.description: Add n to x
entries.commonMistake: For immutables (int, str), creates a NEW object. For list, extends in-place.

entries.description: Subtract n from x
Multiply x by n
entries.commonMistake: list *= 2 repeats the list: [1,2]*2 = [1,2,1,2]

entries.description: Divide x by n (float result)
entries.commonMistake: Result is always float even if divisible evenly: 10/2 = 5.0

entries.description: Floor divide (drops decimal)
Raise x to the power n
Remainder when dividing x by n
Returns the type/class of x
entries.commonMistake: type(x) == int is valid but isinstance() is preferred

entries.description: True if x is an instance of T (or subclass of T)
entries.commonMistake: isinstance(True, int) is True — bool is a subclass of int

entries.description: Returns the memory address of the object x points to
entries.commonMistake: Never use id() for equality checks — use == or is

entries.example: x is None  # idiomatic None check
entries.description: True if x and y point to the SAME object in memory
entries.commonMistake: Use 'is' only for None/True/False and sentinel checks. Use '==' for value comparison.

entries.description: True if x and y are DIFFERENT objects
Removes the name binding. Object freed if refcount → 0
entries.commonMistake: del removes the NAME, not necessarily the object (if other names still point to it)

entries.example: global counter  # use module-level counter inside a function
entries.description: Declare name refers to the module-level (global) variable
entries.commonMistake: global is needed only when you ASSIGN to the name inside the function. Reading a global works without declaring it.

entries.example: nonlocal count  # use enclosing function's count
entries.description: Declare name refers to the enclosing function's variable
entries.commonMistake: nonlocal cannot reach a global variable — only an enclosing function scope.

entries.description: Shallow copy of a list using slice
entries.commonMistake: Shallow — nested lists inside are still shared

entries.description: Shallow copy using .copy() method
Shallow copy using the list constructor
Deep copy — independent at all nesting levels
entries.commonMistake: Required only for nested mutable structures (list of lists, list of dicts)

entries.description: Variables — lowercase_with_underscores
entries.commonMistake: Not camelCase (accountBalance) — that's Java style

entries.description: Constants — UPPER_SNAKE_CASE
entries.commonMistake: Python does not enforce constants — by convention, uppercase means 'do not change'

entries.description: Class names — CapWords / PascalCase
Module-private by convention
entries.commonMistake: Single underscore is convention, not enforced

entries.description: Name mangling — becomes _ClassName__name
entries.commonMistake: Double underscore triggers name mangling inside classes — not just convention

entries.description: Python magic/special methods — do not create your own
These are the type constructors — shadowing breaks list(), dict(), set()
entries.commonMistake: Hardest to spot because PyCharm may not warn you

entries.description: Type conversion functions — shadowing breaks int(), str(), float()
Core built-in functions — shadowing makes them uncallable
Inspection built-ins — id is the most commonly accidentally shadowed
entries.commonMistake: Very common: naming a variable 'id' shadows the built-in id() function
